The Robird is a drone developed by Clear Flight Solutions, that is good for controlling other birds. The Robird looks and flies exactly like a real raptor would, which makes the natural birds react like they would in the presence of a real predator. The Robird is used to clear runways from birds so flights can take off safely, and to keep birds away from industrial or agricultural areas. There are two Robirds built, one looks like a Peregrine Falcon, and the other looks like an Eagle. The Eagle is an alpha predator, while the Peregrine Falcon is the most common predator across the world. Clear Flight Solutions chooses which Robird to use depending on the local bird population. The good thing about using the Robird is that the bird populations quickly learn that a predator is in the area, and stay away. Give the PEAS for the Robird robot.

Describe the PEAS and characteristics of the task environment for the following artificial intelligent agents. i)AI Tutor ii)tennis against a wall iii)Knitting a sweater iv)Bidding on an item at an auction

For each of the following activities, give the PEAS description of the task environment and characterize it in terms of the properties: a) Speech Recognition device b) Satellite Image Analysis system c) Medical Diagnosis d) Performing a high jump

You are given with five different tasks like (i) Crossword puzzle (ii) N Queens Problem (iii) Tower of Hanoi (iv) Ludo List the PEAS description and the properties for the given tasks to their environment characteristics with suitable justification.

For each of the following activities, give the PEAS description of the task environment and characterize it in terms of the properties: a) Speech Recognition device b) Satellite Image Analysis system c) Medical Diagnosis d) Performing a high jump
Illustrate the PEAS components and classify the environment type for the provided scenarios. a) Customer browsing an online shopping website b) Chatbot customer service c) Home automation system

subtopics.name: Introduction to Problem Solving by searching Methods-State Space search
subtopics.title: Introduction to Problem Solving by searching Methods-State Space search

takeaways.text: Understand the concept and structure of state space search in AI.
Learn how to represent and solve problems using state space search methods.

questions.text: Imagine you have three jugs measuring 12 gallons, 8 gallons, and 3 gallons and a water faucet. You can fill the jugs up or empty them out from one to another or onto the ground. How can you measure out exactly one gallon. Represent this as a problem in state space search and extract its production rules.

Formulate the problem definition for a board of size 3*3 with eight numbered tiles and a blank space. A tile adjacent to the blank space can slide into the space. The object is to reach a specified goal state, such as the one shown on the right of the figure.

Formulate the Towers of Hanoi problem with the given initial and goal states. The goal is to move all the disks to another peg, following these rules: i) Only one disk can be moved at a time. ii) A disk can only be moved to a peg that is empty or has a larger disk on top. Draw the complete state space tree for this scenario.

subtopics.name: Uninformed Search Methods – Uniform Cost Search, Breadth First Search, Depth First Search, Depth-limited search, Iterative deepening depth-first
subtopics.title: Uninformed Search Methods – Uniform Cost Search, Breadth First Search, Depth First Search, Depth-limited search, Iterative deepening depth-first

takeaways.text: learn the advantages and disadvantages of different methods
learn how one method overcome the disadvantages of the other

questions.text: Apply the following search algorithms to find a path from the node A to G in the given graph: i) Depth First Search (DFS) ii) Depth-limited search (L=2) iii) Iterative Deepening Search (IDS) Note: Process the nodes alphabetically for the nodes at the same level. Also Analyze the above algorithms regarding completeness, optimality, time complexity, and space complexity

Consider the directed graph below, where a is the start node, and f is the goal node. Which sequences of paths are explored by BFS and DFS in this problem? Show the complete intermediate state space for DFS and BFS with a neat sketch. Would you prefer DFS or BFS for this problem? Justify? Note: Nodes are revisited as per the direction mentioned.

For the given directed graph (start node is S), perform the Uniform Cost Search (UCS). Expand the node based on the alphabetic order if the nodes have the same cumulative cost. 1. Show the priority queue and visited nodes for each step 2. Draw the tree for each step 3. Path and cost to reach the goal states (G1, G2, G3) 4. Find the goal node that has the minimum cost.
Trace the Breadth First Search Algorithm for the given tree structure and Compare its performance with the Depth Limited Search technique.

Consider a 5-city problem: Let's call VIT University (VU) and the 5 cities (C1), (C2),(C3), (C4) & (C5) respectively. A student starts from (VU), he/she visits 5 cities, and then returns back to (VU). Find the state-space representation of this problem. Also, compare DFS & BFS approaches for the given problem.

Consider the initial and the final state for an 8-puzzle problem given below. i. Generate all the states for the given problem ii. Find out the path to the final state using Breadth First Search (BFS) iii. Find out the path to the final state using Depth First Search (DFS)

i.Calculate the number of nodes generated in a depth-limited search to depth l with branching factor n. ii. Calculate the number of nodes generated in an iterative deepening search to depth l with branching factor b. iii. For n = 5, l = 10, suggest which one of the above two algorithms performs better. Justify the answer.

Consider the Water Jug problem as stated here: “You are given two jugs, a 4-gallon one and a 3-gallon one. Neither have any measuring markers on it. There is a pump that can be used to fill the jugs with water. How can you get exactly 2 gallons of water into the 4-gallon jug?” Represent this as a problem in State Space Search and state its Production Rules. Show at least one solution to this problem. Why is ‘depth limited search’ necessary in DFS?
Consider the following graph. In what order will the nodes be visited using a (i) Breadth First Search and (ii) Depth First Search?
Demonstrate the uniform cost search algorithm by using the below graph. Find the minimum cost from node S to node G.
You are designing a search algorithm for a robot navigating a maze. Which search algorithm would be more suitable, Breadth-First Search or Depth-First Search? Justify your choice.

subtopics.name: Informed Search Methods- Best First Search, A* Search
subtopics.title: Informed Search Methods- Best First Search, A* Search

takeaways.text: Understand how heuristic functions guide informed search algorithms.
Learn the differences and applications of Greedy Best-First Search and A* Search.

questions.text: Apply A* Search algorithm to find the shortest path from a to z using the following graph

Trace the operation of A' search applied to the problem of getting to Bucharest from Lugoj using the straight-line distance heuristic. Show the sequence of nodes that the algorithm will consider to reach the goal. Refer to the Hsld mentioned in the table below

Use the Greedy Best-First Search algorithm to solve the Romanian problem. The agent is on holiday in Romania and is currently located in the city of Arad. The agent has a flight from Bucharest tomorrow, so it can't be changed. Now you need to find the shortest path to Bucharest.

Consider the search graph depicted in Figure in which node “1” represents the source node and node “8” represents the goal node. The weight of the edge (i-j) represents the distance between node i to node j. The weights labeled on the top of node i represent the distance from node i to the goal node. Determine the optimal search path from the source node to the goal node using the A* algorithm. Prove that A* is optimal.
State the significance of using heuristic functions. Perform the A* search algorithm on the graph given below taking node ‘0’ as the starting node and giving the proof of optimality.

From the Following Figure. a)What path would the breadth-first graph search return for this search problem? S-G b)What path would uniform cost graph search return for this search problem? S-A-C-G c)What path would the depth-first graph search return for this search problem? S-A-B-D-G d)What path would A* graph search, using a consistent heuristic, return for this search problem? S-A-C-G

Perform A* search and Greedy best-first search on the given graph to find the shortest path from node a to node z. Each edge is labeled with the cost to traverse that edge. Heuristic values are given in the table below the graph.

Differentiate greedy-best first search and A* search by solving the search path from node a to r in the following instance of the graph. The heuristic distance value is given near the nodes. Numbers written on the edges represent the distance between the nodes.
Apply greedy best first search on the following graph and find a path from the start mode (S) to the goal node (E).

subtopics.name: Local search algorithms - hill climbing search, simulated annealing, genetic algorithm
subtopics.title: Local Search algorithms-Hill-climbing search, Simulated annealing, Genetic Algorithm

takeaways.text: Understand the structure and operation of hill-climbing, simulated annealing, and genetic algorithms.
Recognize the strengths and limitations of each local search method.

questions.text: Explain each step of the genetic algorithm with correct terminology in detail using the eight-queen problem and appropriate examples. Calculations must be provided along with the steps when necessary.

Suppose a genetic algorithm uses chromosomes of the form x=abcdefgh with a fixed length of eight genes. Each gene can be any digit between 0 and 9. Let the fitness of individual x be calculated as: f(x)=(a+b)-(c+d)+(e+f)-(g+h) and let the initial population consist of four individuals with the following chromosomes. Evaluate each individual's fitness, showing all your workings and arranging them in order with the fittest first and the least fit last.

With a neat sketch of the hill-climbing algorithm's state-space landscape, explain why the hill-climbing search gets stuck. List the variants of the hill climbing algorithm and describe how they overcome this problem.
Discuss the salient features of local search algorithms and explain their state-space landscape.
Explain the Steepest ascent Hill climbing algorithm. Discuss the situations under which Hill Climbing may fail to find a solution. What can be done to overcome these situations?
Explain the Hill Climbing Search Algorithm. What are the advantages and disadvantages of the algorithm? What is the strategy used in Simulated Annealing that helps it to escape Local Minima?

Elucidate the pseudocode of the simulated annealing algorithm and 'In simulated annealing acceptance probability is designed in such a way that, as the number of iterations increases, the probability of accepting bad performance comes down'- Justify the above statement with a suitable example.

The hill-climbing search algorithm moves to the best successor of the current node according to an objective function. Consider the 8-puzzle problem with the following start state and goal state. Use evaluation function f(n) as 'number of tiles out of place' and solve the following using the Hill climbing algorithm.
Genetic algorithm is the bio-inspired computational intelligence algorithm and it reflects the process of natural selection and genetics -Justify.

A manufacturing plant aims to create an optimal production schedule that reduces production time and efficiently utilizes resources. Discuss how hill climbing could be applied to determine a feasible schedule, highlighting potential obstacles. Also, illustrate how simulated annealing can be used to overcome suboptimal solutions and achieve a better schedule.
Show that hill-climbing approach suffers from local optima in solving a problem. Use a global heuristic function to solve the same problem to reach the goal state.
Explain how simulated annealing can address the plateau problem in hill climbing. Provide an example to illustrate this solution.

A manufacturing facility has a set of jobs to be processed on a set of machines. Each job has a specific processing time on each machine. The goal is to schedule the jobs on the machines in a way that minimizes the total completion time by using the genetic algorithm. Consider that there are 4 jobs (A, B, C, D) and 2 machines (M1, M2). Jobs can be processed on any of the machines. For e.g., job A can be processed on either M1 or M2. The processing times (in minutes) for each job on each machine are given in the following table: [Hint to create the initial population: Chromosome 1: A(M1)B(M2)C(M1)D(M1). The fitness value of this is 3+4+4+5 = 16].
Compare and contrast Hill Climbing search and Simulated Annealing, highlighting their strengths and weaknesses.

subtopics.name: Adversarial Search: Game Trees and Minimax Evaluation
subtopics.title: Adversarial Search: Game Trees and Minimax Evaluation

takeaways.text: Understand how to construct and traverse game trees using minimax evaluation.
Learn the impact and mechanics of alpha-beta pruning in reducing search space.

questions.text: Consider the game tree in which the static scores are from the first player’s point of view. Suppose the first player is a maximizing player. Applying minimax search, show the backed-up values in the tree. What move will the MAX choose? If the nodes are expanded from left to right, what nodes would not be visited using alpha-beta pruning? Also, explain the limitations of the Mini-Max search. How to overcome them?
Perform the minimax algorithm on the figure below

Consider the game tree given below. By Applying a mini-max search, show the backed-up values in the tree. If the nodes are expanded from left to right, what nodes would not be visited using alpha-beta pruning?

subtopics.name: Elementary two-players games: tic-tac-toe, Minimax with Alpha-Beta Pruning
subtopics.title: Elementary two-players games: tic-tac-toe, Minimax with Alpha-Beta Pruning

takeaways.text: Understand how Minimax and Alpha-Beta Pruning algorithms are applied to two-player games like tic-tac-toe.
Learn the process of pruning and its impact on search efficiency.

questions.text: Consider the following two-person game. Apply the alpha-beta algorithm to it and demonstrate the corresponding search tree built by this algorithm. Clearly show the pruned nodes or branches of the tree. Justify that the pruned nodes do not have any impact on the outcome of the game.

Consider the following 4-ply two-person Game. Apply the alpha-beta algorithm to it and demonstrate the corresponding search tree built by this algorithm. Clearly show the pruned nodes or branches of the tree. Justify that the pruned nodes do not have any impact on the outcome of the game.
Explain in detail about the Alpha Beta Pruning technique and solve the given problem using the same to find out all the unvisited nodes in the given tree
Write the pseudocode for alpha-beta pruning. Identify the nodes that are pruned using the alpha-beta pruning algorithm.
Write pseudo/code for Alpha-Beta Pruning. Apply both the minimax algorithm and Alpha Beta pruning for the following graph.

Consider the game tree given below in which the first player is the maximizing player. Applying mini-max search, show the backed-up values in the tree. If the nodes are expanded from left to right, what nodes would not be visited using alpha-beta pruning? Also, explain how pruning is done.

Consider the following two-person game. Apply the alpha-beta algorithm to it and demonstrate the corresponding search tree built by this algorithm. Clearly show the pruned nodes or branches of the tree. Justify that the pruned nodes do not have any impact on the outcome of the game.

Consider the following two-person game. Apply the alpha-beta algorithm to it and demonstrate the corresponding search tree built by this algorithm. Clearly show the pruned nodes or branches of the tree. Justify that the pruned nodes do not have any impact on the outcome of the game.
Find the minimax values of the nodes using the Mini-Max Algorithm. Prune the nodes that do not affect the game's outcome using the Alpha-Beta pruning algorithm.

Consider the game tree in which the static scores are from the first player's point of view. Suppose the first player is a maximizing player. Applying mini-max search, show the backed-up values in the tree. What move will the MAX choose? If the nodes are expanded from left to right, what nodes would not be visited using alpha-beta pruning? Also, explain the limitations of the Mini-Max search. How to overcome them?

Consider a complete binary tree with the leaf nodes 5, 4, 3, 2, 8, 9, 10, 11, 3, 12, 2, 13, 15, 10, 14, 17 (left to right). Consider that tree as the game tree. By Applying mini-max search, show the backed-up values in the tree. Assume min takes the first call. If the nodes are expanded from left to right, analyze the efficiency if alpha-beta pruning is applied?

Consider a two-player turn-based strategy game where each player tries to maximize or minimize the game score at the end of the game. The game tree is given below: a) Apply the Minimax Algorithm to determine the optimal move for the Maximizer at the start node. Show all calculations for each node. b) Apply Alpha-Beta Pruning to the same game tree and show how it reduces the number of node evaluations. Explain which nodes were pruned and why.
You are designing an Al agent to play a real-time strategy game. Discuss the challenges of using Minimax with Alpha-Beta Pruning in this context and suggest potential solutions.

subtopics.name: Introduction to Logic and Reasoning-Propositional Logic
subtopics.title: Introduction to Logic and Reasoning-Propositional Logic

takeaways.text: Understand the fundamentals of propositional logic and its role in AI reasoning.
Learn to formalize puzzles and real-world scenarios using logical expressions and truth tables.

questions.text: Three chests are presented to you. One contains a treasure map, and the other two are empty. Each box has imprinted on it a clue as to its contents; the clues are: Chest 1 'The treasure map is not here' Chest 2 'The treasure map is not here' Chest 3 'The treasure map is in Chest 2' Only one message is true; the other two are false. Which box has the treasure map? Formalize the puzzle in Propositional Logic and find the solution using a truth table
Which of the following are entailed by the given sentence (A||B)&(7C||7D||E)? Justify with suitable rules/laws i) (A||B||C)&(B||C||D=>E) ii) (A||B)&(7D||E) (|| means or, & means and, 7 means not)

Consider the following propositional logic statement: P->(Q&R) i)Prove that the statement P->(Q&R) is logically equivalent to (7P||Q)&(7P||R) using laws of inference. ii) Determine whether the statement (7P||Q)&(7P||R) is a tautology, satisfiable, or a contradiction.
Construct the Truth table for ((P->Q)->R)->S

Consider the Wumpus world shown below: Assume the following knowledge base of the agent.(1,1) → (2,1) → (1,1) → (1,2) → (1,3) → (1,2) → (2,2) → (3,2) Convert the Knowledge base into propositional logic sentences. Show by resolution that, in the given situation, cell (1,4) contains a Wumpus and (3,2) contains a Gold.

Consider the following sentence [(F=>P) and (D=>P)]=>[(F and D)=>P] a.Determine whether this sentence is valid, satisfiable (but not valid), or unsatisfiable, using enumeration. b.Convert the left-hand and right-hand sides of the main implications into CNF, showing each step, and explain how the results confirm your answer to (a). c.Prove your answer to (a) using resolution.

Consider the following set of sentences as a Knowledge Base (KB): Perumal likes all kinds of snacks. Samosa and sandwich are snacks. Perumal eats idly and is still alive. Anything eaten by anyone and is still alive is snack. (i) Convert this KB into First Order Logic. (ii) Now, convert this First Order Logics in Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)

Consider the following sentence: (Rain implies Wet Floor) OR (Dew implies Wet Floor) implies ((Rain AND Dew) implies Wet Floor) (i) Convert the following into propositional logic. (ii) Determine whether this sentence is valid, satisfiable (but not valid), or unsatisfiable? (iii) Convert the left-hand and right-hand sides of the main implications into CNF.

A knowledge base has the following statements: i.If there is gas in the tank and the fuel line is okay, then there is gas in the engine; ii. If there is gas in the engine and there is either a good spark or electronic ignition, the engine runs; iii. If there is power to the plugs or alternate battery charge is present, a good spark is produced; iv. If the battery is charged and the cables are okay, then there is power to the plugs. Convert the rules above to CNF using appropriate proposition symbols such as GasInTank, FuelLineOK, GasInEngine, etc.
Convert these statements to Predicate Logic format. i.People can fool some of the people all of the time. ii. Everyone in the world is loved by at least one person iii. There is at most one child
Prove that (P&Q)->(PVR) is a Tautology using Truth Table
Discuss the limitations of Predicate logic as a tool for Knowledge representation.

Identify the knowledge-type that is easy to represent with semantic network representation. Represent the following knowledge with semantic network structure. a) Every animal is a living thing. b) Every bird is an animal. c) Every bird flies. d) Every bird has 2 legs. e) Every mammal is an animal. f) Every mammal has 4 legs. g) Penguin is a bird. h) Cat is a mammal. i) Bats are mammals. j) Bats have 2 legs. k) Opus is a special-species of Penguin. 1) Bill is cat. m) Pat is a bat.

There are three students, Anjali, Braian, and Chenna. One of them is a topper. When asked, they said the following. Anjali: 'I am not the topper.' Braian: 'I am not the topper.' Chenna: 'Braian is the topper.' If only one person is telling the truth; Who is the topper? Formalize the puzzle in Propositional Logic and find the solution using a truth table.
Explain how Modus Ponen and Modus Tollens can be used to infer logic with a real world example.

Given the following propositional logic formula: (P∧Q)→(R→S) 1) Determine whether this sentence is valid, satisfiable (but not valid), or unsatisfiable. Justify your answer with laws used for inference. 2) Convert the given sentence into CNF, showing each step.

subtopics.name: First Order Logic-Inference in First Order Logic
subtopics.title: First Order Logic-Inference in First Order Logic

takeaways.text: Understand the syntax and semantics of first-order logic (FOL) for knowledge representation.
Learn to convert English statements into FOL and perform inference using forward/backward chaining and resolution.

questions.text: Convert the following English Statements into FOL i) Any house in Parson costs less than any apartment in Chennai. ii) Any small apartment costs less than any big house. iii) There is exactly one house in Parson whose cost is Rs 20,00,000. iv) There is a house in Parson which costs more than any other house.

Given a vocabulary including the following symbols: Profession(p1, p2): Predicate indicating that person p1 holds profession p2. Client(p1, p2): Predicate signifying that person p1 is a customer of person p2. Boss(p1, p2): Predicate expressing that Person p1 is the boss of person p2 Doctor, Physician, Solicitor, Thespian: Constants representing different occupations. Sarah, Alex: Constants referring to specific individuals Use these symbols to write the following assertions in first-order logic i) Sarah is either a physician or a solicitor. ii) Alex is a Thespian, yet he also holds another job. iii) There exists a solicitor all of whose clients are physicians. iv) Each physician has a solicitor.

Represent the following sentences in FOL i) Every student has taken at least one computer science course. ii) A student has taken at most one computer science course. iii) Every student has been in every building on campus. iv) There is a student who has been in every room of at least one building on campus. v) Every student has been in at least one room of every building on campus. vi) Every student has been in every room of every building on campus. Conclusion: (iii) Does (v) entail (iii)? prove it by resolution.

Represent the following sentences in FOL. a. Anything that is played by any student is tennis, soccer, or chess. b. Anything that is chess is not vigorous. c. Anyone who is healthy plays something that is vigorous. d. Anyone who plays any chess does not play any soccer. e. (Conclusion) If every student is healthy, then every student who plays any chess plays some tennis. Use forward chaining algorithm to infer the Conclusion.

Build a vocabulary (constant, predicate, and function), and based on it, write the following assertions in first-order logic: i. Joe is an actor, but he also holds another job. ii. All surgeons are doctors. iii. Joe does not have a lawyer (i.e., is not a customer of any lawyer). iv. Emily has a boss who is a lawyer. v. Every surgeon has a lawyer.
Which of the following are semantically and syntactically correct translations of 'Everyone’s Zip code within a state has the same first digit'? Provide the necessary justification for your answer.

Suppose you have a production system with the three rules: R1: IF A, THEN E R2: IF B AND F, THEN G R3: IF C AND E, THEN F Also, you have four initial facts: A, B, C, and D. Construct an AND-OR graph for the above production system. Show explicitly how it can be used to determine the truth or otherwise, of fact G using 'forward and backward chaining'.

A knowledge base contains the following statements: All hounds howl at night. Anyone who has any cats will not have any mice. Light sleepers do not have anything which howls at night. John has either a cat or a hound. John is a light sleeper. Use modus ponens to conclude that John does not have any mice.

Convert the given knowledge base to FOL. Prove that 'Percy Jackson defeats the minotaur' entails from the given knowledge base using resolution. Knowledge Base: Percy Jackson is a demigod. One becomes a demigod when one's parent is a God. Percy Jackson's parent is the powerful God Poseidon. The children of powerful Gods are powerful. Powerful monsters are imprisoned. The powerful monster Minotaur is imprisoned. Powerful monsters can only be defeated by powerful demigods.

Consider the following statements: 1. Any book in the library is more expensive than any magazine in the store. 2. Any electric car is more efficient and produces less pollution than a gasoline car. 3. There is exactly one student whose GPA is 4.0. 4. There is a restaurant in Paris that serves better food than any other restaurant in the city. 5. A person who helps others is respected by everyone. a) Convert the following English statements into First-Order Logic (FOL) b) Convert the above FOL expressions to Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF).

A. Convert the following English Statements into First Order Logic (FOL) i. Any house in Bhopal costs less than any apartment in Pune. ii. Any 3 star washing machine costs less but consumes more power than a 5 star washing machine. iii. There is exactly one person whose JEE rank is 14. iv. There is a Villa in Mumbai which costs more than any other villa. v. A person who loves his mother is loved by everyone. B. Convert above First Order Logic (FOL) statements to Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF).

Write the logical representation of the following statements: 1. Every student who studies hard will pass the exam. 2. There exists a city such that all its residents are happy. 3. If it's sunny and there's no wind, then the picnic will be enjoyable. 4. There is a student who likes all math courses except for calculus. 5. Every dog has a bone, and every bone has a dog.

subtopics.name: Unification, Forward Chaining, Backward Chaining, Resolution
subtopics.title: Unification, Forward Chaining, Backward Chaining, Resolution

takeaways.text: Understand the principles and algorithms of unification, forward chaining, backward chaining, and resolution in knowledge representation and reasoning.
Learn to apply these techniques to solve logical problems and prove conclusions from knowledge bases.

questions.text: Consider the following story of the 'lucky student': 'Anyone passing his history exams and winning the lottery is happy. But anyone who studies or is lucky can pass all his exams. John did not study but he is lucky. Anyone who is lucky wins the lottery.' Prove using resolution that 'John Is happy'

Consider the following set of rules for identifying animals. *Animals that give milk are mammals *Animals that have feathers, flies, and lay eggs are birds *Animals that eat meat and are mammals are carnivores. *Animals that have black stripes and are carnivores are tigers The working memory contains the following assertions: *A1: Fluffy has black stripes *A2: Fluffy eats meat *A3: Whiskers has feathers *A4: Fluffy gives milk *A5: Whiskers flies i) Use forward chaining to derive ALL assertions that are derivable from this knowledge base (that is, from the set of rules together with the assertions in the working memory). ii) Use backward chaining to determine Fluffy is a tiger. Construct all trees showing the steps followed by forward and backward chaining and show when and how the working memory is updated during the process.

Convert the given knowledge base into Propositional logic statements. Prove 'I will score well' by resolution. Knowledge Base: *If the question paper was leaked, then I can score good marks. *The question paper was not leaked. *If the question paper was set by Stella ma’am, then I can’t score well. *The question paper was set by Stella ma’am or Pearly ma’am. *The question paper was not set by Stella ma’am. *I can score well when Pearly ma’am sets the question paper.

Convert the below sentences in propositional logic and apply resolution rules for propositional logic to find the answer. a. If you have the flu, then you miss the final exam. b. You do not miss the final exam if and only if you pass the course. c. If you miss the final exam, then you do not pass the course. d. You have the flu, or miss the final exam, or pass the course. e. It is either the case that if you have the flu then you do not pass the course or the case that if you miss the final exam then you do not pass the course. f. Either you have the flu and miss the final exam, or you do not miss the final exam and do pass the course. Conclusion: If you have the flu, then you do not pass the course.

Suppose a new pet, called Fotik, is delivered in an opaque (non-transparent) box along with two facts about Fotik: Fact 1: Fotik croaks Fact 2: Fotik eats flies Suppose you have a rule base containing the following four rules:) You can replace X with Fotik wherever applicable) Rule 1: If X croaks and X eats flies - Then X is a frog Rule 2: If X chirps and X sings - Then X is a canary Rule 3: If X is a frog - Then X is green Rule 4: If X is a canary - Then X is yellow a. Using both forward chaining and backward chaining processes to decide whether Fotik is green. b. Explain which is the more appropriate technique to use in this scenario. c. Also draw the AND-OR graph that demonstrates the process of forward chaining.

Use forward chaining and backward chaining to solve for the given knowledge base and goal. Knowledge Base: i. If [X croaks and eats flies] Then [X is a Toad] ii. If [X chirps and sings] Then [X is a canary] iii. If [X is a toad] Then [X is colored brown] iv. If [X is a canary] Then [X is colored yellow] v.[John croaks and eats flies] Goal: [John is colored Y]?

Consider the following sentences in a Knowledge Base (KB): When I feel sleepy, I am happy. If I am at home and AC is on, I feel sleepy. If I am at home and it’s hot outside, AC is on. Today is holiday and I feel sleepy, I am at home. Today is holiday. It’s hot outside. (i) Convert this KB into propositional logic. (ii) Prove that 'I am happy' using backward chaining, with neat graphical and sufficient representation.

Consider the following sentences in a Knowledge Base (KB): When I feel lousy, I am sick. If I am at home and heater is on, I feel lousy. If I am at home and it’s cold outside, heater is on. Today is holiday and I feel lousy, I am at home. Today is holiday. It’s cold outside. (i) Convert this KB into propositional logics. (ii) Prove that 'I am sick' using forward chaining with neat graphical and sufficient representation.

Consider the following Knowledge Base If X have sore throat and cough then X is having Pneumonia If X have pain and fever then X is having Jaundice If X is having Pneumonia then X is Pale white If X is having Jaundice then X is Yellow Richard have sore throat and cough (i) Convert KB into First Order Logic. (ii) With the help of backward chaining prove that Richard is pale white

Consider the following Knowledge Base If Y croaks and eats flies then Y is a Toad If Y chirps and sings then Y is a Canary If Y is a Toad then Y is omnivores If Y is a Canary then Y is a herbivores Z croaks and eats flies (i) Convert KB into First Order Logic. (ii) With the help of backward chaining prove that Z is omnivores.

Animals can be trained by humans to develop cognitive abilities. If an animal can map one event to other, it has cognitive abilities. Trained dogs can associate one particular word to an action. Cassy is a dog trained by his master Jim and he responds to Jim's commands. Using FOL (forward chaining and backward chaining) prove that Cassy has Cognitive abilities.

Five friends have access to a chat room. Either Kevin or Heather, or both, are chatting. Either Randy or Vijay, but not both, are chatting. If Abby is chatting, so is Randy. Vijay and Kevin are either both chatting or neither is. If Heather is chatting, then so are Abby and Kevin. Is it possible to determine who is chatting if the following information is known? By applying Proposition Logic also apply Forward Chaining and Backward Chaining to prove that the derived statements are true/false.

Knowledge Base: Every coyote chases some roadrunner. Every roadrunner who says ``beep-beep'' is smart. No coyote catches any smart roadrunner. Any coyote who chases some roadrunner but does not catch it is frustrated All roadrunners are smart. Prove that 'are all coyotes frustrated'. Convert the knowledge base to FOL and use resolution techniques to answer the query.
Consider the following KB and Solve the questions

Show the result of applying the unification algorithm given in class to each of the following pairs of clauses. Justify the results. (a) P(A,B,B) P(x,y,z) (b) Q(y,G(A,B)) Q(G(x,x),y) (c) Knows(Father(y),y) Knows(x,x) (d) F(Marcus) F(Caesar) (e) F(Marcus,G(x,y)) F(x,G(Caesar,Marcus))

Consider the following sentences: • John likes all kinds of food. • Apples are food. • Chicken is food. • Anything anyone eats and isn’t killed by is food. • Bill eats peanuts and is still alive. • Sue eats everything Bill eats. a) Translate these sentences into FOL. b) Apply both forward and backward chaining to prove that John likes peanuts.

Chuck, Jackie, and Bruce are all members of a martial arts club. Every member of this club is either a follower of kung fu taekwondo or both. No taekwondo follower likes boxing and all kung fu followers like tai chi. Chuck dislikes whatever Bruce likes and likes whatever Bruce dislikes. Bruce likes boxing and tai chi and doesn't like aerial kicks. Use First Order Predicate Logic to represent the scenario and then use resolution  to find out if there is a member of this martial arts club who is a follower of taekwondo but not kung fu?

Consider the following sentences of a knowledge base and prove the given goal using resolution with contradiction method. 1. Every birds sleeps in some tree. 2. Every loon is a bird and every loon is aquatic 3. Every tree in which any aquatic bird sleeps is beside some lake 4. Anything that sleeps in anything that is beside any lake eat fish (Goal) Every loon eat fish.

Consider the following statements of a knowledge base, and prove the given conclusion using forward and backward reasoning. 'All people who are not sick and clever are happy. Those people who read are not fool. Mijan can read and is healthy. Happy people have exciting lives. Therefore Mijan living exciting life'

Consider the following axioms: a) Every child loves Santa b) Everyone who loves Santa loves reindeer c) Rudolph is a reindeer and Rudolph has a red nose d) Anything which has a red nose is weird or is a clown e) No reindeer is a clown f) Scrooge does not love anything which is weird Represent these axioms in first-order logic and prove 'Scrooge is not a child' using forward and backward chaining.

Represent the following sentences using predicate logic. Marcus was a man. Marcus was a Pompeian. All Pompeians were Romans. Caesar was a ruler. All Pompeians were either loyal to Caesar or hated him. Everyone is loyal to someone. People only try to assassinate rulers they are not loyal to. Marcus tried to assassinate Caesar. Prove that 'Marcus hated Caesar' by using resolution and unification.

From 'Horses are animals,' it follows that 'The head of a horse is the head of an animal.' Demonstrate that this inference is valid by carrying out the following steps: a. Translate the premise and the conclusion into the language of first-order logic. Use three Predicates: HeadOf(h,x)(meaning 'h is the head of x'), Horse(x), and Animal (x). b. Negate the conclusion, and convert the premise and the negated conclusion into conjunctive normal form. c. Use resolution to show that the conclusion follows from the premise.

Consider the following axioms: a) Every child loves anyone who gives the child any present. b) Every child will be given some present by Santa, if Santa can travel on Christmas eve. c) It is foggy on Christmas eve. d) Anytime it is foggy, anyone can travel, if he has some source of light. e) Any reindeer with a red nose is a source of light. f) (Conclusion) If Santa has some reindeer with a red nose, then every child loves Santa. Prove the conclusion by resolution.

Jake wants to be selected for a sports team. The selection criteria and Jake's circumstances are as follows: 1. Anyone fast or strong gets selected. 2. Training makes you fast. 3. Exercise makes you strong. 4. Jake trains but does not exercise. Convert the scenario into propositional logic statements and use the resolution to prove that 'Jake gets selected.'

Consider the following set of rules for classifying transportation modes: Vehicles that have engines are motorized. Vehicles that run on tracks and carry passengers are trains. Vehicles that are motorized and have two wheels are motorcycles. Vehicles that have wings and fly are airplanes. Vehicles that are motorized, have four wheels, and carry passengers are cars. The working memory contains the following assertions: A1: Speedster has two wheels. A2: Zoomer runs on tracks. A3: Glider has wings. A4: Speedster has an engine. A5: Glider flies. i) Use forward chaining to derive all assertions that are derivable from this knowledge base (i.e., from the set of rules together with the assertions in the working memory). ii) Use backward chaining to determine whether Speedster is a motorcycle. Construct all trees showing the steps followed by forward and backward chaining, and show when and how the working memory is updated during the process.

Consider the following story of the 'Successful candidate': 'Anyone good at coding or having an innovative mind or having recommendations will get a job. Anyone who practices coding or works in a research project becomes a good coder and innovative. Rahul is too lazy to practice code or work in a research project. However, his uncle will give him a letter of recommendation.' Prove using resolution that 'Rahul will get a job'.

Represent the following assertions using propositional logic: i) If Rahul has fever then Rahul has cough. ii) If Rahul has cough and fever then Rahul is fatigued. iii) If Rahul has headache and fatigue then Rahul has migrane. iv) If Rahul has migrane and fever then Rahul should not look at bright lights. v) If Rahul has cough and migrane then Rahul should rest. vi) Rahul has fever. vii) Rahul has headache. Prove: Rahul should rest using forward and backward chaining techniques.

Represent the following sentences in First-Order Logic (FOL). Use Resolution to infer the conclusion: i) Every member attends every event hosted by the club. ii) Anyone who misses an event is not an active member. iii) Anyone who volunteers at an event is an active member. iv) Anyone who registers for an event either attends it or misses it. v) Emma registers for an event. 'Coding Hackathon' is an event. vi) Emma is a member. (Conclusion): Emma attends the event.

takeaways.text: Understand Bayes' rule and its application in probability reasoning.
Learn to construct and reason with Bayesian Belief Networks (BBNs) for real-world problems.

questions.text: Using the probability values given in the table answer the following statements with proper justifications 1. Does the probability of wind increase given the probability of rain? 2. What is the dependency between wind and Headache? Does the probability of wind remain unchanged given the headache? 3. Is rain conditionally independent of Headache, given wind?
Apply Naïve Bayes Classification for the given dataset of animals along with their attributes. Execute the following query to predict the class

Consider the following dataset: a) Using the provided dataset, which includes demographic information and credit scores of individuals, apply the Naive Bayes classification to predict the credit score of a new individual based on their following demographic features: Age>40, Gender=Male, Income=Medium, Education=Graduate, Occupation=Skilled b) Based on the data given in the table answer the following: i) What is the probability that an individual has a high credit score given that they are Female and have a Postgraduate education? ii) Given that an individual has a High income and a Skilled occupation, what is the probability that their credit score is Low?

Suppose you live in London, England, and you notice that during the winter, it rains 50% of the time and that it is cloudy 80% of the time (sometimes it is cloudy without rain). You know, of course, that 100% of the time, if it is raining, then it is also cloudy. Using Bayes’ rule, compute the chances of rain, given that it is just cloudy.

In standard two of St. John's school, 30% of the children have grey eyes, 50% of them have blue and the other 20%'s eyes are in other colors. One day they play a game together. In the first run, 65% of the grey-eyed ones, 82% of the blue-eyed ones, and 50% of the children with other eye colors were selected. Now, if a child is selected randomly from the class, and we know that he/she was not in the first game, what is the probability that the child has blue eyes?

For the Bayesian network given below: a.Calculate the probability that the door is open, it is wife and not a burglar, also the car is in the garage, and the door is not damaged. b.Calculate the probability that it is the burglar, the door is open and also damaged and there is no car in a garage. c.Calculate the probability that the door is closed, it is not wife and not a burglar, also the car is in the garage, and the door is not damaged.

The following table gives the data on individuals in response to promotional offers made in areas of finance, trade, magazines, and health. Using Bayes's theorem to predict the Sex of the individual for the given data X= {Finance = No, Trade = Yes, Magazines = Yes, Health = No}

Suppose you live in Chennai, Delhi, and you notice that during the winter, it rains 60% of the time and that it is cloudy 70% of the time, sometimes it is cloudy without rain. You know, of course, that 100% of the time, if it is raining, then it is also cloudy. Apply the Bayes rule to compute the chances of rain, given that it is cloudy.

You have a modern Diagnosis system (MYCINE) to predict cancer at your place. It is fairly reliable at detecting cancer that takes place due to pollution but also responds on occasion to happen due to smoking. Also have two distinct findings such as X-ray and Dyspnoea, which have promised to predict cancer. The Bayesian network for this model is given below. Given the evidence of which has or has not to predicted, you would like to estimate the probability of pollution and smoking. What is P(P|X,D) and P(S|X,D)?

While watching an IPL match in a cafe, you observe someone who is clearly supporting CSK in the game. What is the probability that the person is actually born within 30 miles of Chennai? Assume that: * The probability that a randomly selected person in a typical local café environment is born within 30 miles of Chennai is 1/30. * the chance that a person is born within 30 miles of Chennai actually supports CSK is 7/10. * the probability that a person not born within 30 miles of Chennai supports CSK is 2/10.

The entire output of a car manufacturing factory is produced on three assembly lines. The three assembly lines contribute 25%, 35%, and 40% respectively of the total factory output. The percentages of defective cars produced by each of the assembly lines are 5%, 6%, and 2% respectively. If a car is picked up at random from the total output and is found to be defective, what is the probability that it was produced by the second assembly line?

In VIT there are two faculty members who teach Al. One faculty member has a pass percentage of 50% and the other faculty member's pass percentage is 90%. You would want to join the class of the faculty member who has the higher pass percentage. But you do not know who that is. So, you assume that they are equally likely to be lenient and join the class of one faculty member. Given that you failed, what is the probability that the faculty member, whom you chose was faculty with the high pass percentage?
For two events A and B, the following probabilities are given P(A)= 0.6, P(B)= 0.2, P(A|B)=0.5. Use the appropriate laws of probability to calculate i)P(Ā) ii)P(AUB) iii)P(A∩B) iv)P(B|A)
Consider the following Bayesian Network containing four Boolean random variables and answer the following questions

You are a robot in a lumber yard and must learn to discriminate Oak wood from Pinewood. You choose to learn a Bayes rule. You are given the following examples: a) Now assume that the attributes (Density, Grain, and Hardness) are conditionally independent given the Class. Write the posterior probability using this assumption of conditional independence. b) Consider a new example (Density=Light^Grain=Small^Hardness=Hard) and compute these class probabilities.
Information from the patients is given in the table below. Use Bayes’ rule to diagnose a person with the following symptoms [y n mild n]

Suppose you live in London, England, and you notice that during the winter, it rains 50% of the time and that it is cloudy 80% of the time (sometimes it is cloudy without rain). You know, of course, that 100% of the time, if it is raining, then it is also cloudy. Using Bayes’ rule, compute the chances of rain, given that it is just cloudy.

A theme park hired you after graduation. Assume that you want to predict when the theme park receives lots of visitors. You gathered the following data: What is the probability that the learned Bayesian network will predict that the theme park receives lots of visitors on a cloudy and hot weekend day?

You are given two different Bayesian network structures 1 and 2, each consisting of 5 binary random variables A, B, C, D, and E. Each variable corresponds to a gene, whose expression can be either "ON" or "OFF". For each of the following cases, the factor P(A,B,C,D,E) according to the independence specified and gives the minimum number of parameters required to fully specify the distribution. (i) A,B,C,D,E are all mutually independent (ii) A,B,C,D,E follow the independence assumptions of Network #1 above (iii) A,B,C,D,E follow the independence assumptions of Network #2 above (iv) No independencies

Consider The Monty Hall Problem given below; You are a contestant on a game show where you are presented with three closed doors. Behind one of the doors is a car, and behind the other two are goats. The game proceeds as follows: You initially choose one of the three doors, without opening it. The host, who knows what is behind each door, opens one of the other two doors, revealing a goat. You are then given the opportunity to stick with your original choice or switch to the other unopened door. After making your decision, the chosen door is opened to reveal whether you have won the car or a goat. Apply Bayes' Theorem to analyze the probabilities associated with sticking with your initial choice versus switching doors.

An ABC hospital maintains the patient details as follows for diagnosing the patient diseases. Use Naïve Bayes classifier to diagnose a person with the following symptoms [Y N Mild Nj has a Flu or not?

Consider the following dataset that includes demographic information and symptoms for disease diagnosis: a) Using the dataset provided, apply Naive Bayes Classification to predict the diagnosis of a new individual based on the following features: Age Group: 30-40, Gender: Female, Fever: Yes, Cough: Yes, Fatigue: Yes, Sore Throat: No b) Based on the data given, answer the following: i)What is the probability that an individual has a COVID-19 diagnosis given that they are Male and have Fatigue and Cough? ii) Given that an individual has No Fever and No Cough, what is the probability that their diagnosis is Healthy?
Explain Bayes' rule with a suitable example.

In a reputed engineering college, there are 1000 students. Among them, 700 students got placement offers. Overall, it is seen that 82% of the candidates are strong in ‘C language’ among the selected candidates. In the class, 660 students are strong in ‘C language’. Sweta is strong in ‘C language’. How likely is she to get the placement offer? Map the scenario into a set of propositions and apply Bayes' rule to solve the problem.

To prevent break-ins, Harry put a brand-new burglar alarm at his house. The alarm consistently reacts to a break-in, but it also reacts to little earthquakes. Harry has two neighbors David and Sophia, who have taken a responsibility to inform Harry at work when they hear the alarm. David always calls Harry when he hears the alarm, but sometimes he got confused with the phone ringing and calls at that time too. On the other hand, Sophia likes to listen to high music, so sometimes she misses to hear the alarm. Here Compute the probability of Burglary Alarm. i) Calculate the probability that the alarm has sounded, but there is neither a burglary, nor an earthquake occurred, and David and Sophia both called Harry. ii) Calculate the probability of burglary given the alarm. List of all Events occurring in a Bayesian Network · Burglary(B) · Earthquake(E) · Alarm(A) · David Calls(D) · Sophia Calls(S)

A patient presents with symptoms of fever, cough, and fatigue. A doctor wants to diagnose whether the patient has influenza or a common cold. The doctor has prior knowledge about the prevalence of these diseases in the community and the probabilities of various symptoms given each disease. Consider: Influenza (I), Common Cold (C), Fever (F), Cough (Co), Fatigue (Fa) Edges: I → F, I → Co, I → Fa, C → F, C → Co, C →Fa Probabilities: P(I) = 0.2, P(C)=0.8, P(F|I)=0.9, P(F|C)= 0.7, P(Co|I)= 0.8, P(Co|C)=0.6,P(Fa|I)=0.7,P(Fa|C)=0.5 Given the patient's symptoms (fever, cough, and fatigue), what is the probability that the patient has influenza? Solve using Bayes' theorem.
With the given data answer the questions: i) What is the probability of slippery if the season is dry? ii) What is the probability of wet if the ground is slippery?

An agent in Vellore, India wants to spend his vacation in Paris, France. Plan his trip using hierarchical task network planning. Write the actions with their corresponding preconditions and effects in STRIPS.

The Tower-of-Hanoi puzzle consists of three vertical pegs and several disks of different sizes. Every disk has a hole in the middle, and several disks may be stacked on a peg. The rules allow moving disks from peg to peg, one disk at a time; however, the rules do not allow placing any disk above a smaller one. Initially, all disks are on the leftmost peg in ascending order of size, and the task is to move them to the rightmost peg. Consider the puzzle with three disks, and represent it as a planning problem, using the simple STRIPS language. You should encode the initial state, goal, and operators for moving disks.

A monkey is in a room. Suspended from the ceiling is a bunch of bananas, beyond the monkey's reach. However, in the corner of the room is a box. The ceiling is just the right height so that a monkey climbing on the box could pick the bananas and eat them. The monkey knows how to move around, carry other things around, and reach for the bananas. What is the best sequence of actions for the monkey to reach his goal (banana)? Discuss in detail, the planning representation for the above scenario using the goal stack planning.

Formulate the STRIPS representation for the problem of planning a simple robot's path to move a box from one room to another. Define the initial state, goal state, actions, and the preconditions and effects for each action

subtopics.name: Planning as State-space search, Forward search, backward search
subtopics.title: Planning as State-space search, Forward search, backward search

questions.text: Discuss how forward state space search differs from backward state space search. Explain in detail.

Consider a maze with the following layout: Here • is the robot, G is the goal where the robot has to reach, white blocks are open and the shaded blocks are walls. The robot can move one step at a time. Compare and contrast forward search and backward search algorithms for solving this maze navigation problem. Write every step. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each approach.
Discuss how the hierarchical planning helps to reduce the size of the search space with a suitable example.
Explain how hierarchical task network (HTN) planning can be applied in non-deterministic domains. Provide examples to illustrate how HTN planning manages uncertainty and unpredictable outcomes.

subtopics.name: Planning and acting in Nondeterministic domains – Sensor-less Planning, Multiagent planning
subtopics.title: Planning and acting in Nondeterministic domains – Sensor-less Planning, Multiagent planning

takeaways.text: Understand how to represent actions in STRIPS for the blocks world problem.
Learn about planning in nondeterministic domains and multiagent environments.

questions.text: Give generic representations of actions along with the preconditions and effects in STRIPS that can be used in the blocks world problem. Write a suitable plan of action to reach the goal state from the start state. b) Write a short note on planning in a nondeterministic domain.
Demonstrate various planning methods in a non-deterministic domain with suitable examples.

Consider a scenario where multiple autonomous robots are tasked with exploring an unknown environment and mapping its layout. Discuss the challenges and strategies involved in planning and coordinating the actions of these robots in a continuous environment.
Develop an NLP model to create a calendar entry from email received. Explain the approach that you would select to perform the task with a suitable example.

Imagine you're searching for information on a complex topic like 'climate change'. Explain, how search engines would identify pages that are most authoritative and informative pages on this topic without repetitive or irrelevant content with suitable examples.

Consider the problem of image retrieval in a large-scale database of images. Discuss at least 4 challenges involved in image retrieval, the different approaches that can be used to address these challenges. Write the factors that influence the effectiveness of image retrieval systems.

Understand the flow of execution for different branching statements.
Cascaded if-else means using if, else if, and else statements evaluated sequentially.

takeaways.text: Remember the main difference between while and do-while loops.
While and For loops are entry-controlled; Do-while is exit-controlled.
In do-while loop, body executes at least once even if the condition is false.
Learn to convert between while loop and for loop.
Infinite loop declaration using: for(;;).
Break and continue are loop control statements.
Understand flowcharts of all loop statements.
Loop Types:
Differences between break and continue can be directly asked.
Expect questions on loop execution counts and outputs with break/continue.
Application-based questions like finding Prime Numbers or Odd/Even Numbers are possible.

questions.text: Explain differences between for, while, and do-while loops.
Write logic for finding prime numbers using loops.
What is the difference between break and continue?
Convert a while loop into an equivalent for loop.
How many times will the loop run in a given code snippet?

takeaways.text: For an average/below average student, learn all the theory concepts properly with some example codes. Mainly focus on loops and conditionals (with basic examples and flowcharts) and learn theory from type conversions.
Possibility of coding questions like swapping two numbers without a temporary variable and solving quadratic equations using discriminant.
Coding questions from this module won’t be tricky. Just read the question properly to understand what to do. If you don't know exactly what to do, at least write the basic code structure.
As code logic can differ from student to student, it's better to write an explanation of the code (what you are doing) if time permits.
Write sample input and output for every code, as this carries some marks.

If you want good marks from this module, you need to practice the coding part of arrays and strings, which includes using for loops and if statements. (The questions in the exam won't be very tough, but you will need to spend some time thinking about them.)

Entire theory part with basic code examples.

Again, if you think you can’t deal with coding questions, at least learn the basic syntax or basic code structures for every example. (Even if you don't write the exact code, if you write the correct syntax, some teachers may give marks if they want to make you pass).

In the exams, write the expected output after code at last (even though your code logic is wrong, write the expected output as per question). (If you do this, your chance of getting more marks will be increased).

questions.text:
1) Create a structure Student with the following members: Roll_number, Name, Department, Course, Year_of_joining, and Date_of_Birth. Consider implementing Date_of_Birth as a nested structure with Day, Month, and Year. Write a program with:
(a) A function to print names of students who joined in a particular year.
(b) A function to print details of a student by Roll_number.

2) Create a structure called bank with Account_number, Name, Balance, and Address (Address has houseno, city, and pincode as a nested structure). Write a C program with:
(a) A function search() that accepts 'search_pincode' and prints customers with that pincode and balance below Rs. 100.
(b) A function transaction() to update Balance based on Account_number, amount, and transaction_code (1 for deposit, 0 for withdrawal).
3) Define a structure cricket with player name, team name, and batting average. Declare an array player[10] and print a team-wise list with player names and averages.

4) Write a C program that takes user input to store book details (title, price, pages) in a structure. Display the details in ascending order of price (rounded off). If prices are equal, arrange by pages. Use an array of structures. Sample Input: 3 Maths 149.78 442 Physics 149.22 382 Chemistry 149.50 400.

5) Define a structure time_struct with hour, minute, and second. Write a C program to assign values and display the time in seconds (e.g., 1 hour, 1 minute, 1 second = 3661 seconds). Do operations by passing the structure to a function.

6)(A bit tough question) sometimes you can expect these type of questions in cat2 From the scenario, Write a C program that defines a structure named "Device" with pointer attributes including an ID (integer), name (string), and status (boolean). Additionally, define a nested structure called "Light" within "Device," which features pointer attributes such as Watts (integer) and Light_Type (Modern/Classic). Implement a main() function to test the structure variables, utilizing dynamic memory concepts (malloc()/calloc() function) for the pointer attributes in both structures (Device and Light). Furthermore, ensure the main() function reads values for these attributes and prints the results accordingly In Bombay, Jass, a skilled software engineer, was approached by TechGenius, a local startup, to contribute to their smart home automation project, "HomeGenius." Recognising the potential, Jass suggested an object-oriented approach, proposing a "Device" class to serve as the foundation for all devices, with specialised subclasses for specific functionalities. Jass then suggested deriving specialised device classes from the base "Device" class, like such as lights, thermostats, and security cameras. Each device would have common attributes like an ID, name, and status. Jass emphasised the importance of encapsulation, suggesting that private member variables should store device-specific details. Sakthi, TechGenius's CEO, embraced Jass's ideas, acknowledging the benefits of encapsulation and inheritance. They saw how this approach would facilitate scalability and adaptability, allowing for seamless integration of new devices in the future. Together, they aimed to create a system that could efficiently meet evolving technological and customer demands.

takeaways.text: Goal: Maximize the total value in the knapsack.
Unlike 0-1 Knapsack, fractions of items can be taken.

Greedy works here as local optimal = global optimal in fractional cases.

subtopics.name: Divide and Conquer: Maximum Subarray
subtopics.title: Divide and Conquer – Maximum Subarray Sum

takeaways.text: Divide the array into two halves.
Conquer by solving recursively for: maximum subarray sum in the left half, right half, and crossing the middle.
Combine by returning the maximum of the three values.
To find the crossing subarray: find max sum left from mid, max sum right from mid+1, then sum them.
Time Complexity: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) → O(n log n).
Applications: Financial analysis (max profit intervals), Signal processing, Subsequence scoring in bioinformatics.

questions.text: Define the maximum sub-array sum problem. Find the series of contiguous elements that results in the maximum sub-array sum for the array [-2,-3,1,4,-1,3,5,-4,6,1].

The subarray sum S(i,j) is the contiguous subarray with the largest sum. Consider a sequence of 8 elements: A = [-2, 3, 4, -1, -2, 1, 5, -3]. Design an O(n log n) algorithm to find the maximum sum subarray and determine the S(i,j) of A, where 1 ≤ i ≤ j ≤ 8.

subtopics.name: Divide and Conquer:  Karatsuba faster integer multiplication algorithm
subtopics.title: Divide and Conquer – Karatsuba Multiplication

takeaways.text: Karatsuba reduces the number of multiplications from 4 to 3 using clever algebra, improving efficiency for large integer multiplication.
Time Complexity: T(n) = 3T(n/2) + O(n) → O(n^log₂3) ≈ O(n^1.585), much faster than naive O(n²).
Efficient for multiplying large numbers and foundational to fast multiplication algorithms.

questions.text: Design and develop an algorithm to multiply 2 integers and analyze their time complexity. Explain the technique using inputs 1234 and 4321.
Explain the efficiency of Karatsuba time complexity over naive multiplication. Perform multiplication of 1234 * 4321 using Karatsuba algorithm.
Multiply 1234 × 2326 using Karatsuba algorithm. Show valid and invalid recursive calls. Draw the recursion tree.
Design and develop an algorithm to multiply 2 integers and analyze their time complexity. Illustrate with 1334 and 1253.
Use divide and conquer strategy to multiply 38409 and 83329. Derive the recurrence relation and compute the time complexity. Also compute estimated time taken.

takeaways.text: The goal is to determine the most efficient way to multiply a chain of matrices by minimizing the number of scalar multiplications.
Matrix multiplication is associative, so the order of multiplication affects computation time but not the result.
Use a 2D DP table dp[i][j] where dp[i][j] stores the minimum number of multiplications for matrices i through j.
Time complexity: O(n³), Space complexity: O(n²), where n is the number of matrices.

questions.text: Consider a chain of 5 matrices with specific dimensions. Apply dynamic programming to find the minimum number of scalar multiplications needed for (A1.A2.A3.A4.A5) along with the optimal parenthesization.
Given a sequence of matrices <8,5,10,20,5>. Use dynamic programming to compute the minimum scalar multiplications and write the optimal parenthesization.
Provide the optimal parenthesization for multiplying matrices A1, A2, A3, A4, A5 with specified dimensions.
Outline important factors of Dynamic Programming and compute Matrix Chain Multiplication for the array {4, 10, 3, 12, 20, 7}. Compute dp[i][j] where 0 ≤ i, j ≤ 5 with base case dp[i][i] = 0.
Given matrix dimensions: A1: 5x4; A2: 4x3; A3: 3x5; A4: 5x2; A5: 2x4, fully parenthesize A1.A2.A3.A4.A5 to minimize scalar multiplications.

takeaways.text: Goal: Minimize total time to go through all stations from start to end, choosing the optimal line at each stage.
Each step depends on the best previous step, and overlapping subproblems are used in decision making.
Time Complexity: O(n)
Space Complexity: O(n), can be optimized to O(1) using two variables per line.
Applications include manufacturing, industrial automation, and multi-path optimization problems.

questions.text: A car factory has two assembly lines with entry and exit costs, station costs, and transfer costs between lines. Calculate the fastest time to get through the factory and trace the path a car should take for fastest production.
Given n stations on two assembly lines with specific processing and transfer times, implement a dynamic programming solution to compute the minimum total time.
Explain the recurrence relation used in Assembly Line Scheduling and show its application for a 5-station example.
Modify the algorithm to work with 3 or more assembly lines and discuss the changes needed in the dynamic programming approach.

takeaways.text: A subsequence is a sequence that appears in the same order but not necessarily contiguously.
Time Complexity: O(m × n), where m and n are lengths of the two strings.
Space Complexity: O(m × n), but can be optimized to O(min(m, n)) using a rolling array.
LCS has optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems, making it suitable for dynamic programming.
Recursive solutions are inefficient due to repeated recomputation; DP avoids this.

questions.text: Given 2 sequences: X = ABCBADB and Y = BDCBAA. Find the longest common subsequence using dynamic programming. Show all steps in the c table and b table.
Find the longest common subsequence for the strings 'ACCGGTCGAGT' and 'GTCGTTCGG' using a dynamic programming table.
Write pseudocode for the dynamic programming approach to solve the LCS problem and apply it on two strings of your choice.

Given two DNA sequences DNA1 and DNA2, find the minimum number of single-character edits (insertions, deletions, or substitutions) required to transform DNA1 into DNA2. Example: DNA1 = 'AGTACG', DNA2 = 'GTCAGT'.

takeaways.text: Goal: Maximize the total value in the knapsack without exceeding its capacity.
Each item can either be taken once or not at all — that's why it's called 0/1 knapsack.
Time Complexity: O(n × W), where n is the number of items and W is the knapsack capacity.
Space Complexity: O(n × W), can be optimized to O(W) using a 1D array in reverse order.

questions.text: Consider a knapsack bag with n = 4, values = {10, 12, 12, 18}, and weights = {2, 4, 6, 9}. Max weight W = 15. Find the maximum value subset using dynamic programming and derive time complexity.

takeaways.text: Worst-case time complexity: O(N!), due to branching in each row.
Backtracking is used to prune invalid paths early.
Can generate all valid board configurations, not just one.
Applications: Sudoku, Puzzle solvers, Constraint satisfaction problems.

questions.text: Demonstrate N Queen problem for N=5, showing steps with backtracking to generate all solutions where no queens attack each other. Include the algorithm and time complexity analysis.
Explain the N-Queens algorithm on a 4x4 board using backtracking. Draw the State-space Tree with rows and columns numbered 1 through 4.
Define 6-Queens problem. Assume column-wise placement and an intermediate state where queens are attacking each other. Show how backtracking corrects it to a valid solution.
Solve the N-Queens problem (e.g. N = 4, 8) using backtracking and explain it with a valid example.

takeaways.text: Significance of Bounding Function in Subset Sum.
A bounding function is used to prune unnecessary branches in the state space tree.
Reduces the number of recursive calls, making the algorithm more efficient than pure backtracking.
Significantly reduces search space, improving efficiency.
State space tree with bounding has fewer recursive calls, leading to better performance.
Complexity Analysis: Without bounding: O(2ⁿ), With bounding: Much faster in practical cases due to pruning.

questions.text: Given the set of positive numbers {7,11,13,24}, find if there is a subset for a given sum m=31 using backtracking technique. Discuss the significance of bounding function in the context of the above subset sum problem through the full state space tree and highlight the recursive calls on the tree.
Define the subset sum problem. Solve it for the set {40, 30, 50, 10, 20} with a target sum of 60 using the backtracking approach.

takeaways.text: Worst-case Time Complexity: O(m^n) (exponential).
Space Complexity: O(n) (for storing color assignments).
Backtracking explores all possibilities and prunes invalid ones.
Effective for small graphs but inefficient for large graphs.
The algorithm can be optimized using heuristics like MRV (Minimum Remaining Values).

questions.text: Graph coloring is the procedure of assignment of colors to each vertex of a graph such that no adjacent vertices get the same color. Design an algorithm for this problem by applying a backtracking method and color the below graph using red, yellow, and blue (m=3). Show any 10 possible solutions in the state space tree.
Enumerate the possible solutions for coloring the vertices in the below graph using three colors (RGB) using the backtracking approach.

subtopics.name: Branch & Bound: LIFO-BB and FIFO BB methods: Job Selection problem
subtopics.title: Branch and Bound - Job Sequencing and Assignment

takeaways.text: Practice all three methods: LIFO-BB, FIFO-BB, and Least Cost BB method.
Queue helps in systematically exploring the state space tree.
Tuple representation ensures efficient state management.
Priority Queue (Best-First) improves efficiency.
Bound calculation prunes unnecessary computations.

questions.text: Apply the Branch and Bound method to solve the Job Sequencing Problem for the given set of jobs.

Solve the Job Sequencing Problem using FIFO-BB. Select an optimal subset J with an optimal penalty for the given data. What will be the penalty corresponding to the optimal solution? Discuss how a queue is used in the state space tree using fixed tuple representation.

Given the number of jobs n=4, penalties (p1, p2, p3, p4) = (5, 10, 6, 4), deadlines (d1, d2, d3, d4) = (1, 2, 2, 1), and execution times (t1, t2, t3, t4) = (1, 2, 1, 2). Find the actual cost and feasible jobs done using the Branch and Bound technique.

takeaways.text: Backtracking improves over brute force by ignoring infeasible solutions.
Branch and Bound further optimizes by using bounds to prune the search space.
Bound computation estimates the best possible solution for a node’s subtree.
If the bound is worse than the current best, the node is ignored.
Time Complexity: O(2^n).

questions.text: Consider n = 4, V = {10, 10, 12, 18}, w = {2, 4, 6, 9}, and W = 15. What is the highest profit using the Branch and Bound strategy?
Solve the Knapsack Problem using FIFO-BB for knapsack capacity W = 8. Show how a queue is used for node creation in the state space tree.
What is the difference between FIFO-BB and LIFO-BB?
Solve the Knapsack Problem using LIFO-BB for knapsack capacity W = 8. Show how a stack is used for node creation in the state space tree using variable tuple representation.

takeaways.text: Drawbacks of the naive string matching algorithm.
Comparison between the naive string-matching algorithm and the KMP string-matching algorithm.
Drawbacks of the naive string-matching algorithm that are overcome by the KMP algorithm.
Time complexity and space complexity.
Don't go directly to the algorithm; instead, study the conceptual point of KMP and understand the use of the LPS table before studying the algorithm.

questions.text: Find out the longest proper prefix that is also the proper suffix for the given pattern.

Comparison between the naive string-matching algorithm and the KMP string-matching algorithm, highlighting the drawbacks of the naive approach that are overcome by KMP.

takeaways.text: Why should we use Rabin-Karp over KMP algorithm.
Demonstration of rolling hashing function.
Finding spurious hits.
Time complexity and space complexity.

Pseudocode for the Rabin-Karp algorithm (may ask in FAT combination with numerical).

subtopics.name: Bellman-ford
subtopics.title: Bellman Ford Algorithm - Single Source Shortest Path

takeaways.text: Limitation of Dijkstra’s Algorithm.
Principle of Relaxation of Edges.
Detection of a Negative Weight Cycle.
Guarantee of shortest paths without negative cycles.
Time and space complexities.

questions.text: Find the shortest path from source vertex A to all other vertices using a suitable algorithm for the given graph.
Pseudocode of the algorithm.
How Bellman Ford Algorithm is used for detecting negative weight cycles.
