subtopics.name: Floyd Warshall
subtopics.title: Floyd-Warshall Algorithm - All Pair Shortest Path

takeaways.text: Why Floyd-Warshall Algorithm is better for Dense Graphs and not for Sparse Graphs?
Time and space complexities.
Direct numerical-based questions will be asked, solving the problem itself takes more time.

questions.text: Perform a step-by-step process to find the shortest path distance between every pair of vertices using the Floyd-Warshall algorithm, from the directed weighted graph.
Using the appropriate algorithm, find the shortest path between all pairs.

takeaways.text: Uses augmenting paths to find available flow from source → sink.
Can use DFS (or BFS in special cases like Edmonds-Karp).
Keeps updating the residual graph until no more augmenting paths exist.
Works well for small graphs with integer capacities.
Worst case: If floating-point capacities are used, it may run indefinitely.
Time Complexity: O(E * max_flow) (depends on flow value).
Example Use Case: Finding max flow in small networks with bounded capacities.

questions.text: Find the maximum network flow in the graph below and analyze its time complexity.

subtopics.name: Edmond Karp
subtopics.title: Edmonds-Karp Algorithm - Maximum Flow using BFS

takeaways.text: Uses BFS to find shortest augmenting paths.
Edmonds-Karp is Ford-Fulkerson with BFS instead of DFS.
Always finds the shortest augmenting path (in terms of edges).
Guarantees that the number of augmenting paths is O(VE).
Guaranteed polynomial time complexity: O(VE²).
Better for large graphs than standard Ford-Fulkerson.
BFS ensures fewer augmenting paths than DFS, preventing infinite loops.
Example Use Case: Telecommunication networks, road networks, or wireless networks where finding an optimal augmenting path is essential.

questions.text: Write an Edmonds-Karp algorithm, and compute the maximum flow in the network N and analyze its complexity.

In a wireless network, node S wishes to send some packets to node T, but S and T are not directly connected. We need to find the path that can transfer the most packets while satisfying the capacity constraints of the edges. The network is represented as a graph, G = (V,E). Design an algorithm with complexity O(VE²) and find the maximum number of packets transferred from node S to node T using the given graph.
The capacity matrix for a flow network is given below. The first augmenting path found using the Edmonds-Karp algorithm for the graph is 0-1-2-4-5. Is this correct or not? Justify your answer.

subtopics.name: Push Relabel
subtopics.title: Push-Relabel Algorithm - Maximum Flow using Preflow

takeaways.text: Instead of finding augmenting paths, it pushes flow locally using a preflow concept.
Nodes maintain an excess flow, and flow is pushed to neighboring nodes.
Uses relabeling to update height and prioritize where flow should go.
Fast on dense graphs (often outperforms Edmonds-Karp).
Can be implemented using FIFO or highest-label selection for better performance.
Time Complexity: O(V²E) (or O(V³) in worst case).
Example Use Case: Large-scale network flow problems, such as traffic optimization, image segmentation, or airline scheduling.

questions.text: In water distribution systems, we need to find the maximum amount of water that can be supplied from source S to destination T through a network of pipes with capacity limitations. Given a directed graph G=(V,E) representing a water distribution system, where V is a set of vertices and E is the set of edges, each edge (u,v) has a capacity c(u,v) representing the maximum water flow that can be supplied through the network pipe. Use the Push Relabel algorithm to find the maximum water flow that can be supplied from node S to node T using the given graph.

subtopics.name: Line Segments: Properties, Intersection, sweeping lines
subtopics.title: Computational Geometry - Line Segment Intersection

takeaways.text: Understand the cross product of two vectors and how to determine direction.
If p1 x p2 > 0, p1 is clockwise from p2 with respect to the origin.
If p1 x p2 < 0, p1 is counterclockwise from p2.
If p1 x p2 = 0, the vectors are collinear, pointing in either the same or opposite directions.
A line segment intersection occurs if one segment straddles the line containing the other, or an endpoint lies on the other segment.

questions.text: Two line segments intersect if and only if either (or both) of the following conditions hold: 1. Each segment straddles the line containing the other. 2. An endpoint of one segment lies on the other segment (This condition comes from the boundary case.) Given a line-segment L1 with two end-points (2,1) and (6,5), select the other appropriate line segment among the following to justify your answer for the statements (A), (B) and (C) given below. L2 with two end-points (3,5) and (5,2) L3 with two end-points (3,5) and (4,4) L4 with two end-points (7,6) and (8,7) L5 with two end-points (7,6) and (5,8) L6 with two end-points (5,4) and (7,3) Statement A: The condition in line number 5 of the algorithm is "if((d1>0andd2<0)or(d1<0andd2>0))and((d3>0 and d4<0)or(d3<0and d4>0)) return TRUE" Can the "and" operator which is underlined and highlighted in bold, be replaced with the "or" operator to prove that one segment straddles the line containing the other? Statement B: To prove an intersection, is it a must to check the condition "ON SEGMENT(Pi, Pj, Pk)" in line numbers 7 to 14 of the algorithm? Statement C: The else-if conditions in line numbers 7 to 14 of the algorithm show that any one of the directions d1, d2, d3, d4 can be zero to prove the intersection. Is there any line-segment in the list for which only one direction is zero and does not intersect with L1? Is there any line segment in the list for which more than one direction is zero and still does not intersect with L1?

Find whether the following line segments intersect or not using a cross product. a. L1 :{(1,23) & (10,15)} and L2 :{(4,10) & (6,20)) b. L3 :{(4,5) & (7,10)} and L4 :{(1,1) & (5,5)) c. L5 :{(1,1) & (10,10)} and L6 :{(3,3) & (5,5)) d. L7:{(1,1) & (10,10)) and L8 :{(5,8) & (3,3)}

takeaways.text: Overall time complexity is O(n log n) – primarily due to sorting the points by polar angle.
Step 1: Find the lowest point (with the smallest y-coordinate; in case of tie, the leftmost x-coordinate).
Step 2: Sort points based on the polar angle with respect to the base point.
Step 3: Use a stack to build the convex hull.
Step 4: Traverse sorted points and determine left or right turns using the cross product.
Step 5: Pop points from the stack that would make a right turn (not part of the convex hull).
Edge Case:  All points are collinear → convex hull is just the two endpoints.
Edge Case: Duplicate points should be removed before processing.
Edge Case: Precision issues may require handling for floating-point calculations.

questions.text: Given the following set of points 'P' in a plane. Construct the smallest polygon Q such that every point in 'P' is either on the boundary or inside the polygon. Use Graham's Scan Algorithm to wrap the points in counter-clockwise direction: (2,1), (3,3), (5,4), (-2,2), (-3,1), (-4,3), (-1,-2), (-3,-4), (2,-3), (4,-4).
Relate Graham's Scan and Jarvis March Algorithm steps. Discuss how to choose the best practices for computational Convex Hull. Illustrate with an example.

In robotics, path-planning is crucial for efficient navigation. A robot vacuum must clean the edges of a room. How can the Graham Scan algorithm determine the most efficient cleaning path along the room's perimeter? Given coordinates: {(-3,-4), (2,-3), (4,3), (-4,2), (0,5), (2,-3), (-1,4)}.

subtopics.name: Convex Hull: Jarvis’ March Algorithm
subtopics.title: Convex Hull: Jarvis March (Gift Wrapping Algorithm)

takeaways.text: Time Complexity: O(nh), where n → number of points and h → number of points on the convex hull.
Step 1: Start from the leftmost point (with the smallest x-coordinate).
Step 2: At each step, choose the point that is the most counter-clockwise relative to the current point.
Step 3: Repeat until the algorithm returns to the starting point, forming a closed convex polygon.
Step 4: Orientation is determined using the cross product: AB × AC > 0 means C is to the left of AB.
Advantages: Efficient for smaller datasets or when convex hull size (h) is small compared to total points (n).
Disadvantages: Slower than Graham’s Scan for large datasets, sensitive to collinear points.
Applications: Used in computational geometry, robotics, pattern recognition, and collision detection.

questions.text: Write the pseudocode for the Jarvis-March algorithm and trace it for the points provided in the diagram below.
Relate Graham's Scan and Jarvis March Algorithm steps and explain how to choose the best practices for computing Convex Hulls. Provide an illustrative example.

takeaways.text: Randomized algorithms introduce randomness to avoid worst-case scenarios like O(n²) in QuickSort.
Randomized QuickSort selects a pivot randomly, reducing dependency on input order and improving average case performance.
Expected time complexity of Randomized QuickSort: O(n log n).
Space complexity remains O(log n) due to in-place recursive partitioning.
Useful for input-independent and high-performance applications in real-time systems and data processing.

questions.text: Narrate how the Randomized QuickSort algorithm differs from the Normal QuickSort. Write the algorithm and solve it with an example.
Illustrate the need for randomized algorithms. Demonstrate the working of a randomized version of QuickSort with an appropriate example.
Explain how randomization helps prevent worst-case behavior in sorting algorithms.
Derive and explain the expected time complexity of Randomized QuickSort using recurrence relation.

takeaways.text: You interview n candidates in a sequence, and must hire or reject immediately after each interview.
The objective is to hire the best candidate (highest rank) while minimizing the number of hires.
Strategy: Hire the candidate only if they are better than all previously interviewed candidates.
This ensures each hire is strictly better than the previous, reducing the number of total hires.
Expected number of hires when candidates are in random order: O(log n).
If interview cost = ci and hiring cost = ch, then total hiring cost becomes O(ch log n).

questions.text: Write the pseudocode for solving the hiring problem. Consider the cost to interview as ci per candidate and the cost to hire the candidate is ch.
Analyze the performance of the Hire-Assistant algorithm when candidates are presented in random order.
Prove that the expected number of hires in the hiring problem is O(log n).
Explain how randomization influences the efficiency of the hiring problem strategy.

subtopics.name: Global Minimum Cut Problem
subtopics.title: Randomized Algorithms - Global Minimum Cut Problem

takeaways.text: Global Minimum Cut is the problem of finding the smallest set of edges whose removal disconnects the graph.
Karger's Algorithm is a randomized algorithm to solve this problem efficiently.
It works by randomly contracting edges (merging vertices) until only two vertices remain.
The number of edges between the two remaining vertices is the cut size.
Repeat the process multiple times to increase the probability of finding the true minimum cut.
Time Complexity: O(n² log n) for repeated runs to ensure high probability of success.

questions.text: Explain the Global Minimum Cut Problem and how Karger's Randomized Algorithm solves it.
Describe the edge contraction process in Karger's Algorithm and how it maintains correctness.
Why do we need to repeat Karger's Algorithm multiple times? What does it achieve?
Design an implementation of Karger's Algorithm and demonstrate it on a sample undirected graph.

Study branch penalties and logic required to handle branches and interrupts in pipelining.

questions.text: Illustrate a timing diagram for a typical 5-stage pipeline for 9 instructions. Analyze the effect of a conditional branch on the pipeline if instruction 3 branches to instruction 15 with the necessary branch penalty. Derive the logic needed for pipelining to account for branches and interrupts.

Suppose an unpipelined processor with a 40ns cycle time is divided into 5 pipeline stages with latencies of 1,2,4,3, and 5ns. If the pipeline latch latency is 2ns, what is the cycle time of the resulting pipeline stage and pipelined processor?

subtopics.name: Hazards
subtopics.title: Hazards in Pipelining: Types, Prevention, and Drawbacks

takeaways.text: Understand all types of hazards: Data, Control, and Structural.
Analyze methods to prevent and resolve hazards along with their drawbacks.
Practice application-based questions on hazard resolution techniques.
Understand all types of dependencies in superscalar architecture.
Learn the advantages and limitations of superscalar architectures.
Know the differences between superscalar architecture and pipelining.

subtopics.name: Performance evaluation of parallel processors
subtopics.title: Performance Evaluation of Parallel Processors: Amdahl’s Law, Speed-up, and Efficiency

takeaways.text: Practice as many types of questions as possible from this topic.
Understand how to compute speed-up and efficiency using Amdahl’s Law.
Be prepared to analyze the feasibility of a given speed-up value.
Inductive proof is much more important than other types.
Practice questions that involve inductive proof of grammar rules (this is a bit tricky).
Inductive proof of mathematical formulas is easier compared to other types.
Mostly, the examples mentioned in the teacher's PPTs come directly in the exam.

questions.text: Explain the steps involved in an inductive proof with an example.
Compare and contrast deductive and inductive proof techniques.
Prove a given mathematical formula using inductive proof.
Practice inductive proof of grammar rules (e.g., prove that a given grammar generates a specific language).

takeaways.text: Learn how to define grammar for a language.

questions.text: Define the grammar for a given language.
Explain the components of a formal grammar.
How is grammar used to generate strings in a language?

subtopics.name: ALPHABETS,STRINGS,OPERATION ON LANGUAGES
subtopics.title: Alphabets, Strings, and Operations on Languages

takeaways.text: Remember all the definitions with examples.
Learn how to perform operations on grammar and understand what the resultant grammar will be after these operations.

questions.text: Define the terms: alphabet, string, and language with examples.
Explain the different operations that can be performed on languages.
What is the resultant grammar after performing a specific operation?

subtopics.name: Summary
subtopics.title: Module Summary: Proof Techniques and Basics of TOC

takeaways.text: This module has only two main topics: proof techniques and the basics of TOC.
Mainly, focus on learning the definitions of all the concepts mentioned in this module.
Learn the properties of different operations and how to perform various operations on the grammar.
Understand the formal definition and model of Finite Automata.
Understand the concept and working of NFA.
Learn how NFA differs from DFA.
Understand the concept and working of DFA.
Learn how DFA processes input deterministically.

subtopics.name: CONVERSION OF NFA WITH EPSILON TO NFA WITHOUT EPSILON
subtopics.title: Conversion of NFA with Epsilon to NFA without Epsilon

takeaways.text: Understand the concept of ε-NFA and how it differs from NFA.
Learn the step-by-step process to remove epsilon transitions.
Learn the equivalence between NFA and DFA.
Understand the subset construction method for converting NFA to DFA.
Understand the equivalence or partition method for DFA minimization.
Learn the table-filling method and Myhill-Nerode theorem for DFA minimization.
In this module, there are only five subtopics. You need to practice all of them.
Mostly, two questions will come out of these five subtopics in both CAT and FAT.
Sometimes, in minimization of DFA, the DFA won't get minimized. These types are also possible where you follow all the steps, but in the end, the DFA remains the same.
Learn the definition and properties of regular expressions.
Learn how to design regular expressions properly.
Learn Arden's method for converting Finite Automata to Regular Expressions.
Understand the State Elimination method for DFA to RE conversion.
Explore the RIJ K method for conversion.
Practice at least one question in each subtopic.
Don’t get confused with the method names.

subtopics.name: PUMPING LEMMA & CLOSURE PROPERTIES
subtopics.title: Pumping Lemma & Closure Properties of Regular Languages

takeaways.text: Application-based questions from this topic, especially Pumping Lemma.
Learn the proof of the closure properties of regular languages.
For Pumping Lemma problems, take an example of a non-regular language and prove it.

questions.text: Prove that the following language is not regular: L = {a^(n^2) | n≥1} U {a^n b^n | n ≤ 10^(10^5)}.
Show that L = {a^n b a b^(n+1)} is not regular.
The concatenation of two regular languages is regular. Justify why L = {a^n b^n} is not regular despite L1 = {a^n} and L2 = {b^n} being regular.
Prove that the regular set is not closed under infinite intersection.
Prove that L = {w ∈ {a,b} | w has more a’s than b’s} is not regular.
Use Pumping Lemma to determine whether L = {a^i b^(j^2) | i,j ≥ 1} is regular.

Solve for derivatives of a language:
 1) What is L_aa for L = {a^(n+1)b^n | n ≥1}?
 2) What is L_b for L = {a^(n+1)b^n | n ≥1}?
 3) Prove that if L is regular, L_x is also regular.
Check if L1 = {a^p b^q a^r | pq,r ≥1 and p+q ≠ r} is regular.
Prove that regular languages are closed under perfect shuffle.
Use Pumping Lemma to prove L = {w ∈ {0,1}* | w contains more 0’s than 1’s} is not regular.
Use Pumping Lemma to prove L = {w ∈ {0,1}* | w = w^R} is not regular.
Prove that if A is accepted by an NFA and B is not accepted by a DFA, their concatenation is closed under regular languages.

takeaways.text: Be clear about the definition of CFG and practice as many questions as possible on constructing CFG.
Mostly, 5-mark or fewer questions appear from this topic.

questions.text: Write Context-Free Grammar (CFG) for the given language and check if the grammar is ambiguous or not. L = {a^n b^n+m c^m | n, m ≥ 0}.
Construct a CFG for the language L = {(01)^n} U {(10)^n / n≥1}.
Construct context-free grammar for the language: L = {abba(baa)^n aab(aaba)^n | n ≥ 0}.
Design a CFG for the given language Y = {a^n b^m a^2n : n, m ≥ 0}.
Consider the grammar S → 0S1 | 1S0 | λ. Does it generate the language L2 = {w: |w|0 = |w|1}? If not, justify and suggest modifications.
Derive the CFG for the given Context-Free Language and check the ambiguity: L = {v^r i^r+s t^s | r, s ≥ 0}.
Design a CFG for L = {wwRzzR | w, z ∈ {0,1}+, 011 is a substring of w, and |z| is odd}. Explain each production rule.
Design a CFG that generates regular expressions with terminals T = {0,1,(,),U,*,ϕ,ε}. Check if w = (0 U(10)*1)* is accepted by the CFG.

subtopics.name: DERIVATIONS - PARSE TREES - AMBIGUITY IN CFG
subtopics.title: Derivations, Parse Trees, and Ambiguity in CFG

takeaways.text: Direct questions from this topic are common, usually asking to prove that a given grammar is ambiguous.
Focus on constructing derivation trees and checking if multiple parse trees exist for the same input string.
Remember that the CYK algorithm is also called the Membership Algorithm.
If you understand the two given example questions, you can solve most CYK algorithm problems.

questions.text: Determine whether the string w='He eats a fish with a fork' is in the language generated by the given grammar G using the CYK Algorithm.
Consider the following context-free grammar (where A is the start symbol). Apply the CYK algorithm for the input string abbaa. Find the nonterminal set where X is marked in the table.

subtopics.name: SIMPLIFICATION OF CFG – ELIMINATION OF USELESS SYMBOLS, UNIT PRODUCTIONS, NULL PRODUCTIONS
subtopics.title: Simplification of CFG – Elimination of Useless Symbols, Unit Productions, Null Productions

takeaways.text: Practice a few problems on this (mostly direct questions will come).

questions.text: Consider the following context-free grammar (CFG) G =({S, A, B, C, D, E}, (a, b, c, d, e}, P, S) where P is as follows. Apply the steps to simplify the CFG G by removing null and unit productions, then reduce the CFG by removing useless symbols.
Remove all unit productions, useless productions, and null productions from the grammar G=(V,T,P,S). What language does this grammar generate?

subtopics.name: NORMAL FORMS FOR CFG: CNF AND GNF
subtopics.title: Normal Forms for CFG: CNF and GNF

takeaways.text: CNF is more important than GNF.
For a grammar to be converted to GNF, it should first be in CNF. So, whenever a question asks to change to GNF, first check whether the grammar is in CNF or not.

questions.text: Convert the following CFG into an equivalent CFG in Chomsky Normal Form. Consider G={V,T,P,S} where V={S,A,B}, T={a,b}, and P consists of...
Convert the following CFG into Greibach Normal Form. Consider G={V,T,P,S} where V={S,X,A,B}, T={a,b}, S is the start symbol of the Grammar, and P consists of the following...

takeaways.text: Almost direct questions appear in exams. If asked whether a language is Context-Free (CFG) or not, in most cases, the language is not CFG. Prove this using the Pumping Lemma.

questions.text: Given that the language L={z#x | z is a substring of x, where z,x ∈{a,b}*}. Prove that L is not a context-free language.

takeaways.text: Learn the proofs of all closure properties and understand how to apply them in different scenarios.
Practice as many questions as possible from this topic (if you don't have time to practice, just see the answers and understand).
Don't leave the question from this topic if it comes in the exam. Write at least the grammar and extend something, and if possible, explain the logic of your approach.
Go through the example videos mentioned below. If possible, make the videos 2x and just get to know the logic.

questions.text: Compare Finite Automata (FA) and Pushdown Automata (PDA).
Construct deterministic pushdown automata for the given languages and give reasons why we can’t represent the same using Finite Automata.
Create a PDA that recognizes the following context-free language: L = {a∗wc^k | w ∈ {a,b}* and k = na(w)} and give the instantaneous description for the string ‘aababcc’.
Which of the following language(s) is/are accepted by PDA? Design PDA for that language using an empty stack. Also, explain why the rest of the language(s) is/are not accepted by the PDA.
If a language is accepted by PDA through an empty stack, then the same language cannot be accepted by PDA through a final state. Prove or disprove the above statement with justification.
Construct a Pushdown Automata for the following language and display whether the string w = bbbbaaccdd is accepted or not: L = b^2n a^4 c^n d^2, n >= 1.
The language L has strings of balanced '#' and '@' symbols. Derive the language for the given condition and construct the PDA for the derived language.
Design a PDA for accepting the language L = {a^m b^n c^p d^q | m + n = p + q: m, n, p, q >= 1}. Show the moves made by the PDA for one valid and one invalid input string.

Construct a PDA that can identify strings in the language L, defined as those containing sequences of 'a's, 'b's, and 'c's, where the counts of 'a's and 'b's are equal, or the counts of 'b's and 'c's are equal, with each count being greater than or equal to zero.

Design a PDA to accept the language L = {a^2 b^2n c^n | n >= 1}. Show that the string 'aabbbbcc' is recognized by the above PDA by drawing possible instantaneous descriptions during the processing of the strings.
Construct a PDA for the following language L = {0^2k 2^4i 3^i 1^k | k, i >= 1} and check for the acceptance of the string w = 0002222311 using the above PDA.
Construct a PDA for the given language and show the moves made by the PDA for the following strings: (a) aabbbbcc, (b) abc. L = {a^n b^(n+m) c^m | n, m >= 1}.
Construct a Pushdown Automata (PDA) for the following language, L = {0^n 1^m 2^(m+n) | m, n >= 0}, and display whether the string w = 0011122222 is accepted or not.

takeaways.text: Block diagram of microprocessor
Difference between i3, i5, i7
Difference between microprocessor and microcontroller
Difference between 8085 (8-bit) and 8086 (16-bit)
Difference between Von Neumann and Harvard Architecture
Follow any extra topics mentioned by your teacher.

questions.text: Explain the block diagram of a microprocessor.
Differentiate between i3, i5, and i7 processors.
What is the key difference between a microprocessor and a microcontroller?
How do 8085 and 8086 processors differ in terms of architecture and operations?
Compare Von Neumann and Harvard architectures with examples.

takeaways.text: Questions may focus on architecture or pin diagram of 8051.
Memorize all bits in the Program Status Word (PSW) register.
Understand how PSW changes after arithmetic operations.
Learn the program for adding two 16-bit numbers in 8051.
How to change register bank using Program Status Word (PSW).
Understand the default space allocated for the stack in memory.
Direct questions on RAM and ROM organization may appear in exams.
Cover all topics explained in the video.
Be prepared for questions on loop execution counts and time delay calculations.
Nested loops (usually three levels) are often used in time delay calculations.
Understand how to write code to generate a given time delay.
Differentiate between single-loop and nested-loop implementations for delays ≤255 and >255.
Be prepared for direct questions on explaining all addressing modes.
Understand Immediate, Register, Direct, Indirect, and Indexed addressing modes.
Identify the addressing mode from a given piece of code.
Understand how values move between register banks using the stack.
Learn how to use the Program Status Word (PSW) to change register banks.
Familiarize yourself with PUSH and POP instructions in the 8051 microcontroller.
Understand how to perform arithmetic operations on multiple data values (e.g., adding 35H, 76H, 8AH, 97H, 92H).
Learn how to perform arithmetic operations on data stored at specific memory locations (e.g., adding data stored at 200H).
Familiarize yourself with ADD, ADC, SUBB, MUL, and DIV instructions in the 8051 microcontroller.
Remember that all logical operations in 8051 are bitwise operations.
Be prepared to analyze logic circuits, derive expressions, and write corresponding assembly code.
Understand how to generate logical operations using AND, OR, XOR, CPL, and SWAP instructions.
Sometimes, you may be given a Karnaugh Map (K-map) and asked to write code for its minimized expression.
Branching statements (conditional & unconditional) are essential in almost every 8051 program.
Understand different jump instructions (e.g., LJMP, SJMP, AJMP) and their use cases.
Learn conditional branching instructions like JZ, JNZ, JC, JNC, DJNZ, and how they control program flow.
Expect debugging-type questions, especially from this module and port programming (Module 4).
If coding is unclear, memorize model codes (only 3-4 main codes) and understand where to change values.
Time delay using loops follows a standard structure—know when to use which type and how to calculate delay.
In stack operations, learn to move values between different register banks using PSW values.
Understand the theory and syntax for addressing modes.
Be familiar with moving values from ROM to RAM using MOVC, MOVX instructions.
Arithmetic operations may involve adding values from memory locations or solving given expressions.
Expect logic-based programming questions, such as calculating area, perimeter, or solving expressions.
In questions, input and output ports may be mentioned directly or inferred by reading carefully.
Always move FFH to the input port through the accumulator to indicate it as an input port.
Learn how to use JB, JNB, SETB, and CLR commands in port programming.
A single bit cannot be moved directly from one port to another. Use carry: MOV C, P2.7 | MOV P1.0, C.
Byte data can be moved directly between ports: MOV P2, P1 is correct.
The basic code structure for timers remains the same; only TMOD and TH1/TL1 or TH0/TL0 values change.
If the code is unclear, memorize it and understand where to modify values for calculations.
Learn about the TMOD and TCON registers.
Understand Timer Mode 2 and its differences from Mode 1.
Know how to write code for different duty cycles.
Counters are similar to timers, but their TMOD values differ slightly.
Practice questions like counting cars in a parking lot and sending the count status to a port.
The basic structure of counter codes is similar to timers; only certain register values change.

questions.text: 1) Develop an Assembly Language Program (ALP) using interrupts to do the following: (a) Generate a 10 KHz frequency on P2.1 using To 8-bit auto reload (b) Use timer 1 as an event counter to count up 1-Hz pulse and display it on P0. The pulse is connected to EX1 Assume that XTAL = 11.0592 MHz.

2) The car parking area can accommodate a maximum of 100 cars. Assume that a sensor is connected to P3.4 of 8051 microcontroller to sense the car entering the parking area. If the count reaches 100, the microcontroller should turn 'ON' a buzzer connected to P1.7 for 1 second. Assume the crystal frequency as 22 MHz. Write a suitable 8051 assembly code to implement.

3) An 8051 microcontroller based system is designed to count the students entering into a class room of capacity 60, where an enable switch is connected to the microcontroller port which helps the faculty to start the counting process. After 3 minutes of the counting process, a motor which is connected to the P2.1 should be turned 'ON' to close the door by sending 'HIGH' value to P2.1. Also the final count value after 3 minutes should be sent to P1. Develop an ALP for the above scenario.

4) Develop an 8051 ALP to generate 10 KHz frequency using timer 0 in order to blink the serial lamp (connected to P2.1) used to decorate the hall. Imagine a sensor is connected with the pin T1 to count the number of entries inside the hall. For this one can feed the input by connecting a switch at pin T1 in which each turning off of switch indicates the number of incoming persons entering into the hall. Count the number of persons & display the value on port 0. When the count becomes FFH then turn on 8 LEDs connected to P0.

5) Assume that two sensors are connected to P3.4 and P3.5 of the 8051 Microcontroller. Sensor connected to P3.4 is placed near entry door of the auditorium and another sensor is connected to P3.5 is placed near the exit door. The maximum capacity of the auditorium is 200. Write an ALP to find the number of people inside the auditorium and send the count to port P0. An LED connected to P1.7 will glow if the auditorium is full.
6) Develop an ALP for 8051 Microcontroller to initiate with counter 1 in mode 2. When the count reaches 25, turn on the buzzer for 1 second which is connected in port pin P1.2.

7) Assume a switch is connected at P1.2. Develop an ALP for 8051 based on the status of the switch. If the SW = 0 then generate a square wave of 2KHz using timer 0 and if SW = 1, then generate a square wave of 5KHz using timer 1.
8) Write an 8051 assembly language program to create a waveform with a 33% duty cycle on P0.2 using Timer. Assume the delay and Provide the necessary calculations.
9) Write an 8051 (AT89C51) ALP to generate a pulse waveform with a frequency of 2KHz using a timer. Don't access the TLO or TL1 register in the ALP. Provide the necessary timer calculations.

10) Write an 8051 ALP language which consists of a delay module DELAYX which can create a delay of x time units. Generate a periodic pulse waveform with frequency F = 1/(4x) and 50% duty cycle using DELAYX. Don't assume the number of machine cycles for any instruction.

11) Find the delay (D) generated by the timer-0 if it has the following settings TMOD = 01H, THO = 00H and TL0 = 00H. Write 8051 ALP to generate a pulse waveform with frequency f =1/(2D) and duty cycle = 50% using timer-0 and simultaneously transfer data from Port-0 to Port-1. Assume XTAL = 11.0592 MHz.

takeaways.text: The basic serial communication code structure remains the same.
Baud rate changes require modifying the TH register value.
For a baud rate of 19200, remember to use the PCON register.

questions.text: An 8051 microcontroller operating with crystal frequency of 11.0592 MHz is connected to a switch at P1.0. Write an ALP to monitor the status of switch for every 1ms after successful transmission of the message. If the switch is open, then transmit a string “PLACE TO LEARN” stored at ROM location of 200H at 9600 baud rate. If the switch is closed, then transmit a string “CHANCE TO GROW” stored at ROM location 400H at 4800 baud rate. Use timer for the delay generation. Assume the overhead delay caused due to transmission of string is negligible.
Develop a program to receive data until the received data is “N”, means if 8051 receives “N” then it should stop receiving the character. Baud rate of 9600.

takeaways.text: Learn the code for interrupts involving different combinations (Timer & Serial, Timer & External, etc.).
Understand how to handle multiple interrupts simultaneously.
Familiarize yourself with the IE (Interrupt Enable) and IP (Interrupt Priority) registers.

questions.text: 1) Develop an Assembly Language Program (ALP) using interrupts to do the following: (a) Generate a 10 KHz frequency on P2.1 using To 8-bit auto reload (b) Use timer 1 as an event counter to count up 1-Hz pulse and display it on P0. The pulse is connected to EX1 Assume that XTAL = 11.0592 MHz.

2) Develop an Assembly Language Program (ALP) using interrupts to do the following: (a) Receive data serially and send to P1 (b) Read port 2, transmit data serially, and give a copy to P0 (c) Make timer 0 generate a square wave of 2 KHz frequency on P3.6 Assume that XTAL = 11.0592 MHz. Set the baud rate at 9600.

3) Develop an 8051 based ALP for the following scenario: Imagine a sensor is connected with the pin P3.2 (INT1) of 8051 to count the number of entries inside the hall. For this, you can feed the input by connecting a switch at P3.2 in which each CLOSING of switch indicates the number of incoming persons entering into the hall. Count the number of persons and display the value in Port1. When the count becomes 100 transmit the message 'FULL' serially at the baud rate of 4800.

4) Write an assembly language program in which the 8051 gets data from P1 and sends it to P2 continuously while incoming data from the serial port is sent to PO. Assume that XTAL=11.0592 MHz. Set the baud rate at 9600.

5) Assume 8051 Microcontroller is used in a highway toll collection system. There are two sensors connected to 8051: one for entry (at INTO) and the other for exit (at INT1). Whenever a vehicle enters the billing station, the sensor at the entry point sends an active low signal to the system and this will enable the system to generate a maximum delay using timer1 in mode2. After this delay, the sensor at the exit will show an active low signal to the system and this will generate a maximum delay using timer0 in mode 2. Develop an ALP to implement this scenario.

takeaways.text: LCD is more important than LED and is likely to appear in both CAT and FAT exams.
Questions often involve displaying messages on the LCD, potentially in different modes.
Some questions may combine LCD interfacing with serial communication or mathematical operations.
Be prepared for questions that involve displaying values stored in ROM on the LCD.

questions.text: Develop an 8051 assembly program for a 2x16 LCD display. Assume 'VITVELLORE' is stored in ROM starting from 200H. Bring the characters to be displayed in LCD from ROM location starting at 200H.

Design an 8051-based LCD display system with data lines of LCD connected to Port 0 and control signals to Port 1. Develop an assembly language program for the same system to display 'VIT'. (Note: '38H'-LCD 2 lines 5X7 Matrix; '0EH'-Display ON cursor ON; '01H'-Clear LCD '06H'-Shift cursor right)

Write an 8051 assembly language program to interface a 16x2 LCD by sending Hex code commands '38H, 0EH, 01H, 06H' and display 'VIT Vellore' on it in line 2 position 3. Assume D0-D7, RS, R/W', and E pins of LCD are connected with P1.0-P1.7, P2.0, P2.1, and P2.2 pins of 8051 microcontrollers, respectively.

takeaways.text: Questions will come indirectly and involve seven segment display applications.
The code for seven segment display follows similar patterns but requires specific calculations before displaying on the LED.
Be prepared to display numbers on two seven segment displays connected to different ports.

questions.text: Write an 8051 ALP to find numbers divisible by both 3 and 7 in the range 1 to 99 and display them on two seven-segment displays connected to P0 and P1.
Write an 8051 ALP to multiply two numbers without using MUL AB instruction and display the product on seven-segment displays connected to P0 and P1.
Write an 8051 assembly language program that gets 8-bit data from P0, sends it to P1, and turns on an LED connected to P2.5 for 100 microseconds when P3.3 (INT1) goes low.

takeaways.text: Mostly direct questions will be asked where we need to write the Assembly Language Program (ALP).
Sometimes row and column values will be given, and we need to find which key is pressed (for 3 or 5 marks).

questions.text: Develop an assembly language program to interface a 4x4 matrix keyboard with 8051 microcontroller.

Develop an 8051 assembly program such that P1.0 to P1.3 are connected to the rows and P2.0 to P2.3 are connected to the columns of a 4x4 keypad, detect, identify the pressed key and display the ASCIl value of the pressed key at Port PO.

takeaways.text: Learn the theory part and remember the connection diagrams for both ADC and DAC interfacing.
Questions typically come indirectly, often in the context of sensor applications.
The basic code structure and algorithm remain consistent, but specific calculations may be required based on the application.

questions.text: Assume that a Temperature Sensor is interfaced to 8051 microcontroller to sense the temperature of the room. Design a block diagram and a circuit diagram to explain the interfacing of the mentioned sensor and its conditioning with 8051 microcontroller.
Draw the diagram for interfacing 8051 with DAC 0808 at Port P1, develop 8051 assembly programs to generate (a) a stair-step ramp waveform and (b) a triangular waveform.

Draw the block of the described system with the required control signals. A temperature sensor is connected to the analog input of an ADC, the 8-bit digital output lines of the ADC are connected to P1 of 8051 and the 8-bit input of the DAC is connected to P0 of 8051. Write 8051 ALP to continuously get the data (C) from the ADC output, compute F = (9*C)/5 + 32 and send the result (F) to input of the DAC.

Draw the connection diagram of ADC 0804 to interface with the 8051 Microcontroller such that the data lines of ADC are connected to P1 of 8051, RD signal at P2.5, WR Signal at P2.6 and INTR signal at P2.7. Write an assembly language program for the above connection to get the digital output.

takeaways.text: This module is entirely theoretical with emphasis on understanding ARM architecture fundamentals.
ARM architecture and processor modes are likely to appear as 7 or 10 mark questions in exams.
Topics like registers, pipelining, vector table, and exception handling typically appear as supplementary questions.
Focus on understanding the differences between different processor states and between 3-stage and 5-stage pipelining architectures.
Learn all block diagrams thoroughly, including data flow models, pipeline structures, and register organization.

questions.text: Describe the ARM dataflow model and registers with a block diagram and explain the operations of individual blocks.
Explain the 3-stage pipelining architecture of ARM7 and the 5-stage pipelining architecture of ARM9 processors with relevant diagrams depicting their pipeline structures.
A pipeline is a mechanism a RISC processor uses to execute Instructions. Explain with a neat diagram the three-stage pipeline that is incorporated into the ARM 7 processor.
With a neat diagram, explain the data flow between the ALU and the Barrel shifter in the ARM processor.
With a neat diagram, explain the data flow between the ALU and the Barrel Shifter in the ARM processor.
Discuss the different processor modes of ARM processors with neat sketches describing their register banks.
Explain with a neat diagram the three stages and five stages pipeline that is incorporated in the ARM processors.

The ARM core uses RISC architecture. RISC is a design philosophy aimed at delivering simple but powerful instructions. Describe about the four major design rules on which the RISC philosophy is implemented.

takeaways.text: ARM7 supports 7 addressing modes including immediate, register, and various indirect modes crucial for efficient memory access[2][5].
Conditional execution uses CPSR flags (N,Z,C,V) updated by ALU operations with 'S' suffix instructions[7][9][12].
Load-store architecture uses 3-address format for data processing instructions with 32-bit fixed-length encoding[4][6].

questions.text: Explain any 10 condition attributes (condition execution) that controls whether or not the ARM core will execute an instruction.
Explain with examples the different addressing modes for single register Load-store ARM instructions.
Discuss the function performed in the ARM Processor by this instruction ADD r3,r5,r12
Use the ARM instruction set of ARM7 TDMI and write a code to get three times of value/data in R1 as the result.
Write a simple ARM program to find y=x²+3x+5 where x is input stored in r0 register
Provide a concise overview of the data processing instructions available for ARM processors.
Discuss the different addressing modes for single register Load-store instructions of ARM 7 microprocessors.
Draw the CPSR register contents of the ARM 7 processor and explain condition flags updated by ALU operations[9][11].
